# Watering and Irrigation

## Drip Irrigation System

### Equipment (from DripWorks, Willits, CA)

- **Order number**: DW-2026-88412
- **Main supply line**: 1/2-inch polyethylene tubing, 100 ft roll — $18
- **Drip emitter line**: Netafim TechLine CV 17mm, 12-inch emitter spacing, 0.6 GPH — 200 ft roll — $42
- **Timer**: DIG BO9D battery-operated digital timer (2 programs, 4 start times per program) — $28
- **Fittings**: Tees, elbows, couplings, end caps, figure-8 end clamps — $12
- **Pressure regulator**: 25 PSI, 3/4-inch hose thread — $8
- **Filter**: 150 mesh Y-filter — $7
- **Total system cost**: $115 (including shipping)

### Layout

Each raised bed has two parallel runs of drip emitter line, spaced 16 inches apart. The emitters are positioned to deliver water directly at the root zone, approximately 6 inches from each tomato stem. Total emitter count across all beds: approximately 190 emitters at 0.6 GPH each.

### Installation

Kevin plans to install the system on April 12, three days before transplanting. The main supply line connects to the outdoor spigot on the south side of the house via a Y-splitter (one side for drip, one side for hose). The timer attaches directly to the spigot.

## Watering Schedule

### Establish Phase (April 15 — May 15)
- Water deeply every 2-3 days for 30 minutes per zone
- Goal: encourage roots to grow deep into the raised bed soil
- Monitor soil moisture with finger test (moist at 2-inch depth)

### Growing Season (May 16 — September 30)
- **Morning run**: 6:00 AM, 25 minutes every other day (approximately 1 inch per week)
- **Adjust for heat**: When daytime temps exceed 90F (typically late July through mid-August in Portland), increase to daily watering for 20 minutes
- **Target**: 1-1.5 inches of water per week total (including rainfall)
- Portland averages less than 1 inch of rain per month from July through September, so drip irrigation is the primary water source during peak growing season

### Rain Barrel Supplementation
- Two 55-gallon rain barrels collect from the garage roof downspout
- Use rain barrel water for hand-watering seedlings and spot-watering any plants showing stress
- Rain barrels typically fill from October through June; they provide supplemental water in early summer before they empty

## Mulching

- **Material**: Straw (not hay — hay contains weed seeds). Purchased from Concentrates NW in Milwaukie, OR. One bale ($12) covers all four beds.
- **Depth**: 3-4 inches around each plant, keeping mulch 2 inches away from stems to prevent rot
- **Benefits**: Reduces evaporation by approximately 25%, moderates soil temperature, suppresses weeds, prevents soil splash (which can spread blight spores to lower leaves)
- **Application timing**: Apply mulch immediately after transplanting and drip line installation
